title: Cornish currant biscuits
subtitle: Crisp, spiced biscuits loaded with currants
excerpt: Crisp, lightly spiced biscuits made with lard or butter and packed with currants, rolled to about 5 mm and cut into rounds. In the tradition of Cornish heavy cake and similar West Country currant biscuits, the fat and sugar are rubbed into the flour before the currants and a little milk go in. They bake to a deep golden brown and have a satisfying snap and a clean, not-too-sweet flavour.
sourceNotes: Currant biscuits and spiced currant pastries are documented in Cornish and West Country baking literature from at least the eighteenth century. Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747, public domain and available at Project Gutenberg), describes spiced currant pastries as a standard British bakery item. Cornish heavy cake (a slab version of this style) and related currant biscuits are described in domestic cookery books from the West Country published before 1928.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled biscuits in an airtight box. Defrost at room temperature.
recipe.batchNotes: Double the recipe easily. Bake in batches on lined trays.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Store in an airtight tin for up to 5 days. They crisp further on the second day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 170°C. Bake for 16 to 20 minutes until deep golden. Watch carefully: the currants can scorch before the biscuit is fully done if the oven is too hot.

glossaryTerms.definition: Working cold fat into flour with fingertips until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs, with no visible lumps of fat. The movement is a lifting, pinching motion rather than kneading: lift the fat-and-flour mixture, rub it between thumb and fingers, and let it fall back into the bowl. Cold hands and cold fat are important; warm hands melt the fat rather than coating the flour particles. For Cornish currant biscuits, the rubbed-in mixture should hold together when squeezed in a fist before the liquid is added.

content.text: These are a simple biscuit made with the rubbing-in method:

until crumb-like, then bound with a little milk and egg. The currants go in after the rubbing-in stage so they stay intact. Rest the dough for 30 minutes in the refrigerator before rolling: it relaxes the dough and makes it easier to roll thin without springing back.

Sift the flour, mixed spice, baking powder, and salt into a large bowl. Add the cold butter and rub in with fingertips until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs. Stir in the sugar and currants. Beat the egg with the milk and add to the mixture, stirring with a fork until a dough just comes together; add a little more milk if needed. Wrap and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 190°C conventional (170°C fan). Line two baking trays with baking paper. Roll the dough on a lightly floured surface to about 5 mm thick. Cut into rounds with a 6 to 7 cm cutter and place on the lined trays with a little space between each. Re-roll the offcuts. Bake for 16 to 20 minutes until deep golden. Transfer to a rack immediately and cool completely.
